reasoning: The Federal Reserve is currently in a pausing/cutting cycle. The primary credit discount rate (DPCREDIT) fell to 4.50% in January 2025 and has held steady through May 11, 2025. Market expectations generally forecast further rate cuts or indefinite pauses throughout 2025, rather than rate increases. A rate hike within a week (by May 18) or a month (by June 10) is practically impossible absent an unprecedented inflationary shock and an emergency inter-meeting hike (or a surprise hike at a June meeting, which usually happens mid-June anyway). Over the longer horizons (August and November), the probability slightly increases as there is a marginal chance that inflation resurges dramatically in late 2025 forcing the Fed to reverse course and raise rates, though this remains highly unlikely based on current Wall Street consensus (which expects rates to end the year around 3.5%-3.75%). Therefore, probabilities start at the minimum 0.02 and gradually increase to 0.10 for the November resolution date to reflect growing time-horizon uncertainty.
